label: battery
description: Lithium resource extraction and manufacturing is the process of producing lithium compounds from various sources that contain lithium, such as lithium ores (spodumene, lepidolite, etc.), brines (continental, geothermal, oilfield brines), and clay resources. The main objective of this process is to convert lithium into compound forms like lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃), lithium hydroxide (LiOH), lithium chloride (LiCl), and lithium metal to meet the high demand in the electric vehicle and battery industries. Related patents focus on methods of efficiently extracting lithium through various processes such as physical and chemical separation, evaporation, leaching, and electrochemical separation. For instance, there are methods of mixing organic solvents to extract lithium from brine, generating lithium eluate solutions using ion exchange reactors, and manufacturing lithium hydroxide through multi-stage filtration systems. Additionally, techniques for mixing, crushing, and surface modification for producing active materials for lithium secondary batteries are also included. Such technologies maximize the purity and efficiency of lithium and contribute to improving productivity through customized processes based on resource characteristics. This classification includes innovations in lithium resource extraction and battery material manufacturing but excludes the production of completed lithium-ion batteries or non-commercial uses of lithium resources.
example_papers: Electrochemical cell, e.g., solid-state battery, comprises solid-state electrolyte, anode, and interfacial layer between the solid-state electrolyte and the anode, where interfacial layer comprises lithium metal sulfide

Lithium-metal negative electrode used in lithium-metal liquid-electrolyte electrochemical cell, comprises negative-electrode base layer comprising base-layer metal including aluminum and titanium, protection layer arranged on and supported by negative-electrode base layer

Silicon nanostructure-based anode used for lithium-ion battery for e.g. portable electronics, toys and cars, consists of composite of amorphous polysilicon or crystalline silicon-containing films having preset thickness

Lithium cell used for lithium metal battery, comprises electrolyte material, cathode structure arranged on one side of electrolyte material, cathode structure including cathode electrode and cathode current collector, and anode structure arranged on opposite side of electrolyte material

Making anode for electrochemical energy storage device, involves mixing silicon particles and at least one polymer to form mixture, coating mixture onto copper current collector to form coated copper current collector and subjecting coated copper current collector to temperature treatment

Energy storage device electrode, comprises silicon substrate with one or more trenches, each of trenches has trench base with electrically active surface with electrically insulated sidewalk and field regions, active silicon surface

Nanocomposite used in energy storage device, comprises set of dispersed nanoparticles comprising core and shell and are configured to absorb and release lithium ions, and set of electrically coupled carbon particles

Composition of matter used as electrode in electrochemical cell e.g., electrochemical cell, fuel cell, or lithium-ion battery, comprises composite comprising reduced compound, where reduced compound comprises particles, particles comprises grain embedded in silicon dioxide, grain comprises silicon

Solid electrolyte material used for electrolyte of lithium-ion battery, comprises lithium, phosphorus and sulfur as constitutent element, and amounts of constitutent element satisfies specified relation

Battery electrode composition comprises population of composite particles, comprising silicon and carbon, population is particle size distribution as determined by laser particle size distribution analysis

children.label: lithium_upstream_process
children.description: The Lithium Upstream Process technology refers to the process of extracting and refining lithium from lithium ore (hard rock type) or brine resources, serving as a precursor stage for the production of lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃) or lithium hydroxide (LiOH) for batteries. The hard rock upstream process primarily involves mining lithium-containing ores such as spodumene and lepidolite, followed by processes such as crushing, grinding, classification, and flotation (or magnetic separation) to produce concentrates. These concentrates are then treated using either the Acid Roast Method or Lime Roast Method to produce lithium sulfate (Li₂SO₄) solutions. The brine upstream process involves pumping lithium-containing brine from underground, concentrating it at large-scale evaporation ponds, removing impurities (such as Na, Ca, Mg, SO₄, etc.), and converting it into lithium compounds through carbonation, re-dissolution, and compositional transformation. Recently, efforts to enhance recovery rates and reduce climate and location dependency have spread with the application of Direct Lithium Extraction (DLE) technology. The core objective of the lithium upstream process is to produce high-purity intermediates (such as lithium sulfate and lithium chloride) from low-grade ore or brine to supply the downstream process (lithium salt conversion and crystallization). The hard rock method has lower initial investment costs and quicker market response but is associated with high mining and energy costs and greater environmental burden. In contrast, the brine method has lower operating costs and allows for by-product recovery, but the recovery rate is low, around 10-20%, and requires large evaporation ponds and extended concentration times. Overall, the lithium upstream process provides the foundational key pre-treatment technology for battery-grade lithium compound production by applying optimized mining, concentration, and refining techniques depending on the form and grade of lithium resources. In the future, the introduction of high-efficiency and low-environmental-impact processes (such as DLE, wastewater recycling, and energy-saving roasting technologies) is expected to be crucial for securing competitiveness.

children.label: lithium_upstream_ore
children.description: Lithium Upstream-Ore technology involves the extraction and purification of lithium from hard rock ores, such as spodumene and lepidolite, as well as ore resources like clay and jadarite. Traditional resources like spodumene are known for their high lithium content (up to 8%) and established processing methods; however, there has been active development of extraction technologies for non-traditional hard rock ores and clay resources, such as lepidolite, petalite, and jimbaldite, to diversify supply chains. The general stages of ore upstream processing consist of crushing/flotation, calcination, leaching, and refining. The sulfuric acid method is primarily used for spodumene, where it is mixed with sulfuric acid after high-temperature calcination to alter its crystal structure. In contrast, lepidolite and clay minerals can extract lithium without calcination, or under relatively lower temperatures due to their layered silicate structure. The advantages of this technology include shorter production times compared to brine sources, less climate influence, and relatively lower capital expenditures (CAPEX), allowing for flexible responsiveness to market demand. Notably, clay or jadarite have softer properties compared to rocks, presenting potential for energy savings in beneficiation processes like crushing. However, there are drawbacks, such as high energy consumption and operating expenses (OPEX) during ore mining and high-temperature processing, leading to significant amounts of tailings and slag that pose environmental burdens. Additionally, non-traditional ores have lower lithium grades compared to spodumene, necessitating more precise impurity removal and process optimization. Recently, the focus of ore upstream processing has been on developing environmentally friendly extraction processes, such as low-temperature processes and the use of NaOH. Overall, lithium upstream-ore technology is a key technology that extends from a spodumene-centric focus to include non-traditional ores and clay resources, securing a stable supply of lithium for batteries. The critical factors for future commercialization will be improving recovery rates from low-grade ores, enhancing energy efficiency, and increasing profitability through the valorization of by-products produced during the process, such as feldspar and calcium silicate.

children.label: spodumene_processing
children.description: Lithium spodumene processing technology is a fundamental processing technique aimed at securing precursors of lithium compounds used in downstream processes, utilizing spodumene (lithium aluminum silicate), a representative hard rock lithium mineral. Spodumene is the most widely used lithium ore globally, establishing itself as a key raw material in the current lithium industry due to its relatively high lithium content and stable supply. Spodumene processing generally begins with mining, followed by crushing and classification, and then proceeds to flotation or magnetic separation to produce concentrated spodumene with enhanced lithium grade. The goal of this stage is to separate lithium-bearing minerals from unwanted gangue minerals such as quartz and feldspar, thereby improving efficiency in subsequent processes. Since the spodumene concentrate obtained from beneficiation has low chemical reactivity, it undergoes a calcination process where the mineral's crystal structure is altered through high-temperature treatment. During this process, the dense α-spodumene structure is converted into the more reactive β-spodumene structure, enabling lithium to be chemically extracted more easily. After calcination, a leaching process utilizing sulfuric acid is predominantly applied. Sulfuric acid is reacted with β-spodumene to produce a lithium sulfate solution, which is then purified through solid-liquid separation and impurity removal processes. The resulting lithium sulfate solution is the final product of the upstream process, which is subsequently converted into lithium compounds for batteries, such as lithium carbonate or lithium hydroxide, in the downstream process. Although alternative processes, such as lime methods, are applied in some cases, the sulfuric acid method is the most widely utilized in the industry. The most notable feature of spodumene-based upstream processes is their shorter processing time and higher lithium recovery rates compared to brine-based lithium production methods, allowing for a rapid response to changes in market demand. However, high energy consumption due to mining and high-temperature calcination, along with the large amounts of tailings and slag generated during the beneficiation process, remain challenges to be addressed. Consequently, recent efforts have been made to optimize process conditions for energy savings in calcination, utilize low-grade ores, and develop resource recycling technologies that repurpose by-products for construction materials. Overall, spodumene processing technology currently constitutes the core of the global lithium supply chain and is expected to evolve towards not only simple lithium extraction but also improvements in energy efficiency, reduction of environmental burdens, and utilization of by-products in the future.

children.label: lepidolite_processing
children.description: The lithium - hydrometallurgical processing technology of lepidolite, a non-traditional hard rock lithium mineral, involves processing lepidolite (a mica group lithium ore) as raw material to secure lithium compound precursors. Lepidolite has a layered silicate structure and an average lithium content of about 3.0-4.1%, which is lower compared to spodumene; however, it is characterized by its relatively wide distribution and some physical properties similar to clay minerals. The lepidolite processing process fundamentally follows the common flow of hard rock ore hydrometallurgy, including stages of crushing, concentration, leaching, and refining, but the process conditions are designed differently due to variations in mineral structure and reaction characteristics. First, the mined ore undergoes crushing and classification to control particle size, followed by concentration of lithium-containing minerals through flotation and other beneficiation processes. However, lepidolite has a lower grade compared to spodumene and contains a lot of mica, thus requiring more ore to secure the same amount of lithium production, resulting in relatively high amounts of tailings and slag. In the subsequent stages, lepidolite is known to enable lithium extraction through acid leaching without calcination due to its mineral structure. Accordingly, some processes optimize conditions to skip the calcination step or simplify it under low-temperature conditions, thereby reducing energy consumption and carbon emissions. In the leaching process, sulfuric acid is primarily used to leach lithium into a lithium sulfate solution, which is then purified through solid-liquid separation and impurity removal to secure a lithium-containing solution as the output of the hydrometallurgical process. The advantages of lepidolite-based hydrometallurgy lie in its potential for energy savings due to the possibility of reducing the calcination process and diversifying lithium supply sources through the utilization of non-traditional resources. On the other hand, the increase in processing volume due to low ore grade, the complexity of the leaching and refining processes from impurity components (such as aluminum and potassium), and the issue of handling large amounts of tailings and slag act as technical and economic burdens. To overcome these limitations, recent efforts have been focused on optimizing leaching conditions, developing selective impurity removal technologies, and utilizing byproducts as construction materials through resource circulation process technologies. Overall, lepidolite processing technology is seen as a means to complement spodumene-centered lithium hydrometallurgy, possessing both potential for energy efficiency improvement and process simplification; however, securing economic viability due to low grade remains a key challenge for commercialization. With the anticipated increase in lithium demand and the growing scarcity of high-grade ores, the strategic importance of lepidolite processing technology is expected to gradually expand.

children.label: lithium_clay_processing
children.description: Lithium clay/other processing technology is a hydrometallurgical technology that secures lithium compound precursors using clay-type lithium minerals such as Hectorite and Illite, as well as other non-traditional hard rock ores as raw materials. Clay-type ores have a very low lithium content of about 0.2-0.4%, but they easily break down in water due to their sedimentary properties and apply similar processes to hard rock development methods, excluding the crushing process. Although commercial processing methods for clay-type lithium have not been fully developed yet, a recent low-temperature sintering process utilizing hydrogen sulfate and sulfates as additives has been developed, achieving a high lithium leaching rate of over 85% without the introduction of strong acids. This process recovers lithium through water leaching after sintering at relatively low temperatures of 350-750℃, while reducing the acidity of the leachate to prevent equipment corrosion and reduce subsequent process costs. The key challenge in clay/other ore processing is to secure economic viability due to extremely low grades and the issue of processing large amounts of tailings. However, as the scarcity of high-grade lithium resources increases in the future, the strategic importance of technologies utilizing these low-grade resources is expected to gradually expand.

children.label: lithium_upstream_brine
children.description: Lithium Upstream-Brine technology is a pretreatment process that uses lithium-containing brine as a raw material to produce intermediate products such as lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃) and lithium hydroxide through concentration and purification processes. Brine resources are primarily sourced from the lithium triangle in South America (Chile, Argentina, Bolivia) and geothermal/oil field brines, with process design varying according to lithium content and impurity composition. The representative methods are categorized into evaporation method and DLE (Direct Lithium Extraction). The evaporation method is a traditional process that involves the long-term (12 to 18 months) evaporation and concentration of brine in large solar evaporation ponds to increase lithium concentration, followed by the removal of impurities (such as Na, K, Mg, Ca) and the subsequent carbonation and crystallization. This method has a simple setup and low operating costs (OPEX), but it is highly dependent on climate and location, with recovery rates limited to 40-50%. DLE is a technology that selectively extracts lithium and includes methods such as adsorption, ion exchange, membrane separation, electrochemical processes, and solvent extraction. The adsorption method uses alumina and titanium-based adsorbents to selectively capture and desorb lithium, while ion exchange uses strong acidic cation exchange resins to remove impurities. The membrane method simultaneously performs concentration and purification utilizing nanofiltration and reverse osmosis, and the electrochemical method separates lithium ions through electrolysis and electrodialysis. The solvent extraction method selectively elutes lithium using organic solvents. DLE boasts recovery rates exceeding 80-90%, excellent impurity removal efficiency, and lower climate dependence, reducing production time, but it involves high initial investment costs (CAPEX) and process complexity, with some technologies still in early commercialization stages. Recently, the DLE-Pond hybrid method has emerged, which involves pre-concentrating brine using evaporation ponds before recovering high-purity lithium via DLE, achieving both production volume control and cost reduction. Moreover, there is a trend toward establishing modular DLE platforms and post-process collaboration systems to develop customized production systems that can accommodate various brine raw materials. In conclusion, lithium upstream-brine technology is a key pretreatment technology for producing high-purity lithium intermediates from low-concentration, high-impurity brine. For future competitiveness, high-recovery, low-energy DLE technologies, hybrid concentration systems, and byproduct recycling will be essential.

children.label: lithium_evaporation_and_concentration_method
children.description: The evaporation method is a traditional process that utilizes solar energy to evaporate and concentrate saline water in large evaporation ponds over a long period (12 to 18 months) to increase lithium concentration, followed by the removal of impurities (such as Na, K, Mg, Ca, etc.) and carbonation/crystallization. This method features simple equipment and low operating costs (OPEX), but it has a high dependence on climate and location, and the recovery rate is limited to around 40-50%.

children.label: lithium_salts_dle
children.description: DLE is a technology for the selective extraction of lithium, which includes methods such as Adsorption, Ion Exchange, Membrane Separation, Electrochemical Process, and Solvent Extraction. The adsorption method selectively collects and desorbs lithium using alumina and titanium-based adsorbents, while the ion exchange method removes impurities through strong acid cation exchange resins. The membrane method performs concentration and purification simultaneously by utilizing nanofiltration and reverse osmosis, and the electrochemical method separates lithium ions through electrolysis and electrodialysis. The solvent extraction method selectively elutes lithium using organic solvents. DLE has a high recovery rate of over 80-90%, excellent impurity removal efficiency, and low climate dependence, which can shorten the production period; however, it has high initial investment costs (CAPEX) and process complexity, and some technologies are still in the early stages of commercialization. Recently, the DLE-Pond hybrid method has emerged, which concentrates brine in advance using evaporation ponds and then recovers high-purity lithium through DLE, achieving both production volume control and cost reduction. Additionally, there is a trend to establish a modular DLE platform and a post-process collaborative system to develop customized production systems that respond to various brine feedstocks.

children.label: lithium_downstream_process
children.description: The Lithium Downstream Process technology refers to the process of manufacturing final lithium products from intermediates (lithium sulfate, lithium phosphate, lithium chloride, lithium metal, etc.) or byproducts obtained during the upstream process. The primary goal of the downstream process is to produce battery-grade lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃) or lithium hydroxide (LiOH), passing through stages of chemical conversion, purification, and crystallization according to the product's application. The lithium sulfate (Li₂SO₄) solution produced from ore-based upstream processes is converted into lithium carbonate through carbonation reactions or into lithium hydroxide via causticization reactions. In this process, auxiliary materials such as Na₂CO₃ and Ca(OH)₂ are used, and purification technologies such as filtration, washing, ion exchange, and bipolar electrodialysis (BPED) are applied to remove impurities (Na, K, Mg, Ca, Fe, etc.). In the brine-based downstream process, concentrated brine after impurity removal is carbonated to crystallize lithium carbonate, or lithium hydroxide is produced directly through BPED. The core of the downstream process is to ensure high purity of the products and to improve process efficiency. Battery-grade lithium compounds must have impurity levels reduced to below ppm levels, necessitating multi-stage purification and crystallization processes. Existing methods are limited by high usage of auxiliary materials and significant waste generation (Na₂SO₄, CaCO₃, etc.), leading to high costs and environmental burdens. Recently, technologies have been developed to minimize the use of auxiliary materials, recycle waste, and recover and reuse byproducts (sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid, etc.) to reduce costs. Overall, the lithium downstream process constitutes the final manufacturing stage that converts intermediates produced from the upstream process into high-purity lithium products for various industries such as batteries, ceramics, and lubricants, and is a critical technology that influences product quality and production costs. To secure competitiveness in the future, low-energy, low-waste conversion technologies, high-efficiency purification and crystallization systems, and the utilization of byproducts in a circular manner are essential.
example_papers: Electrochemical cell, e.g., solid-state battery, comprises solid-state electrolyte, anode, and interfacial layer between the solid-state electrolyte and the anode, where interfacial layer comprises lithium metal sulfide

Lithium-metal negative electrode used in lithium-metal liquid-electrolyte electrochemical cell, comprises negative-electrode base layer comprising base-layer metal including aluminum and titanium, protection layer arranged on and supported by negative-electrode base layer

Silicon nanostructure-based anode used for lithium-ion battery for e.g. portable electronics, toys and cars, consists of composite of amorphous polysilicon or crystalline silicon-containing films having preset thickness

Lithium cell used for lithium metal battery, comprises electrolyte material, cathode structure arranged on one side of electrolyte material, cathode structure including cathode electrode and cathode current collector, and anode structure arranged on opposite side of electrolyte material

Energy storage device electrode, comprises silicon substrate with one or more trenches, each of trenches has trench base with electrically active surface with electrically insulated sidewalk and field regions, active silicon surface

Nanocomposite used in energy storage device, comprises set of dispersed nanoparticles comprising core and shell and are configured to absorb and release lithium ions, and set of electrically coupled carbon particles

Composition of matter used as electrode in electrochemical cell e.g., electrochemical cell, fuel cell, or lithium-ion battery, comprises composite comprising reduced compound, where reduced compound comprises particles, particles comprises grain embedded in silicon dioxide, grain comprises silicon

Solid electrolyte material used for electrolyte of lithium-ion battery, comprises lithium, phosphorus and sulfur as constitutent element, and amounts of constitutent element satisfies specified relation

Battery electrode composition comprises population of composite particles, comprising silicon and carbon, population is particle size distribution as determined by laser particle size distribution analysis

Battery comprises separator separating anode and cathode, and lithium electrolyte, where cathode comprising active powder plus binder, active powder does not have atomic layer deposition coating, and anode comprising coated graphite powder and binder

children.label: lithium_downstream_lithium_carbonate
children.description: The Lithium Downstream – Lithium Carbonate technology is a downstream process that converts and refines lithium intermediate products (e.g., lithium sulfate solution, lithium chloride solution, low-purity lithium carbonate, etc.) produced in upstream processes into battery-grade high-purity lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃). The core of this process is to remove impurities (such as Mg, Ca, Na, K, B, Fe, etc.) contained in low-purity lithium carbonate (Technical Grade, TG) or lithium solutions, while controlling particle size, crystal form, and purity to stably produce lithium carbonate that meets the specifications for secondary battery cathode materials (Battery Grade). The typical Lithium Downstream – Lithium Carbonate technology consists of carbonation, purification, and crystallization stages. The precipitation method, which involves adding sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) to lithium solutions to precipitate lithium carbonate, is the most commonly used method, forming the mainstream of commercial processes due to its advantages of low energy consumption and process complexity. However, to secure battery-grade quality, it typically requires two or more steps of re-dissolution and re-precipitation (double purification), which can lead to increased costs and scaling issues. Consequently, recent technological developments are focused on enhancing precipitation efficiency and simplifying purification stages through improved mixing uniformity, reaction control using CO₂ gas injection, and optimization of stirring, temperature, and concentration conditions. To achieve high purity, processes such as filtration, washing, re-dissolution, and crystallization are applied following precipitation, along with auxiliary purification techniques such as solvent extraction, ion exchange, and bipolar electrodialysis (BPED) as needed. Through these processes, low-purity lithium carbonate (with a purity of around 90-95%) can be converted into high-purity lithium carbonate (above 99.5%), and controlling crystal size and shape ensures suitability for battery processes. Furthermore, recently, resource-recycling processes that reclaim high-purity lithium carbonate by recycling residues or deposits generated during the lithium carbonate manufacturing process have gained attention. In summary, the Lithium Downstream – Lithium Carbonate technology is a key downstream process that determines the added value of products through the conversion from low-purity to high-purity, assessed as a critical factor for commercial competitiveness due to the expansion of the battery market, which involves cost reduction in purification, scaling suppression, and simplification and continuous improvement of processes. In this regard, the following subsequent questions may also be helpful.
example_papers: Battery comprises separator separating anode and cathode, and lithium electrolyte, where cathode comprising active powder plus binder, active powder does not have atomic layer deposition coating, and anode comprising coated graphite powder and binder

children.label: lithium_hydroxide_manufacturing
children.description: Lithium-hydrothermal-hydroxide lithium (LiOH) technology is an advanced conversion process that uses lithium sulfate (LS) or lithium phosphate (LP), which are intermediates in the upper stream, as raw materials to produce battery-grade high-purity lithium hydroxide, essential for high-performance high-nickel cathode materials. The core of this technology is Bipolar Membrane Electrodialysis (BPED), which selectively separates lithium ions through electric energy, achieving ultra-high purity products with impurity levels reduced to one-third of the conventional methods. Notably, the process incorporates a resource recycling structure that minimizes waste generation by reusing the sulfuric or phosphoric acid produced within the system and secures both process shortening and economic viability through a direct conversion method that omits the lithium carbonate intermediate step. Ultimately, achieving over 80% lithium recovery rate and high-quality stability is assessed as a key factor influencing commercialization competitiveness in the global high-end battery materials market.

children.label: lithium_halve_process_lithium_sulfide
children.description: The lithium‑hydrothermal‑lithium sulfide (Li₂S) technology is a conversion and purification process for producing high-purity lithium sulfide from lithium salts/compounds. Lithium sulfide is a key raw material for the synthesis of sulfide-based solid electrolytes, and higher purity enhances ionic conductivity and synthesis quality, thereby influencing the performance and stability of all-solid-state batteries. The industry aims for a target purity of 99.99%, with particle size control and uniformity being key technical challenges. The solvent method involves mixing lithium hydroxide with an organic solvent and injecting hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) gas to produce lithium sulfide, allowing for the production of high-purity products at levels of 99.995%. However, addressing the toxicity issues of solvents and gases is necessary. The high-temperature gas-solid sintering method manufactures lithium sulfide by reacting lithium and sulfur compounds in a high-temperature gaseous atmosphere, enabling process time reduction and continuous production, but key factors include ensuring particle uniformity and reaction control. The carbo-thermal reduction method involves reacting metal oxides with carbon at high temperatures to remove oxygen and obtain lithium sulfide, allowing the use of low-grade raw materials, though cost-saving technologies for high-temperature processes are required. In summary, the lithium‑hydrothermal‑lithium sulfide technology is a crucial downstream process that maximizes the performance of all-solid-state batteries through quality, purity, and particle control of the solid electrolyte raw materials. The realization of cost reduction and environmentally friendly processes is assessed as the key to competitive advantage in the future.
example_papers: Electrochemical cell, e.g., solid-state battery, comprises solid-state electrolyte, anode, and interfacial layer between the solid-state electrolyte and the anode, where interfacial layer comprises lithium metal sulfide

Solid electrolyte material used for electrolyte of lithium-ion battery, comprises lithium, phosphorus and sulfur as constitutent element, and amounts of constitutent element satisfies specified relation

children.label: lithium_metal_production
children.description: Lithium-hydrothermal-metallic lithium (Li-metal) technology is a conversion process that manufactures metallic lithium using lithium salts or lithium compounds (e.g., lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃), lithium chloride (LiCl), lithium hydroxide (LiOH)) as raw materials. Metallic lithium is regarded as a key raw material in advanced energy materials such as secondary battery anodes and solid electrolyte batteries. A representative manufacturing method is the electrolysis of molten salts, where high-purity lithium salts (LiCl, Li₂CO₃, etc.) are melted at high temperatures and lithium metal is extracted through electrolysis. Recently, a process has been developed that uses lithium carbonate as a feedstock and electrolyzes it in a LiCl-KCl molten salt, which can reduce raw material costs and energy consumption compared to existing LiCl-based processes, while improving current efficiency to around 80-90%. Additionally, the introduction of environmentally friendly solvents (such as ethanol) in wet metallurgy and closed-loop processes is enhancing the sustainability of high-purity LiCl purification and metallic lithium production. The advantages of metallic lithium manufacturing technology include the production of high-purity products, improved process energy efficiency, and diversification of raw materials (such as lithium carbonate and lithium hydroxide); however, challenges such as energy consumption due to high-temperature processes, the hygroscopic nature of LiCl, and handling difficulties have been identified as technical issues. Recently, process innovations through low-temperature electrolysis, raw material purification optimization, and waste reduction have strengthened the commercialization competitiveness of metallic lithium. Overall, the metallic lithium hydrothermal process is evaluated as a key conversion technology that determines cost competitiveness and quality stability in the next-generation battery materials market.
example_papers: Lithium-metal negative electrode used in lithium-metal liquid-electrolyte electrochemical cell, comprises negative-electrode base layer comprising base-layer metal including aluminum and titanium, protection layer arranged on and supported by negative-electrode base layer

Lithium cell used for lithium metal battery, comprises electrolyte material, cathode structure arranged on one side of electrolyte material, cathode structure including cathode electrode and cathode current collector, and anode structure arranged on opposite side of electrolyte material

Energy storage device electrode, comprises silicon substrate with one or more trenches, each of trenches has trench base with electrically active surface with electrically insulated sidewalk and field regions, active silicon surface

Material used in anode of lithium battery cell, comprises lithium metal foil having lithium foil thickness and comprising lithium metal and crystalline defects containing lithium and other element chosen from hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen

children.label: other_lithium_downstream
children.description: The Other Lithium Downstream technology refers to the application technologies that manufacture new lithium products using non-traditional resources such as lithium process by-products or wastewater, in addition to the major lithium products. For example, this includes recovering lithium compounds such as lithium carbonate (Li₂CO₃) or lithium hydroxide (LiOH) from wastewater, sludge, or residual solutions generated during lithium processing, or modifying the residual slag after lithium extraction to be used as construction materials such as concrete admixtures. Recently, technologies have been developed that utilize a bipolar membrane electrodialysis (BPED) process to simultaneously capture carbon dioxide (CO₂) while selectively concentrating and recovering lithium ions to continuously produce high-purity lithium carbonate. Moreover, resource recycling processes that recover valuable metals such as magnesium and calcium phosphate at high purity from lithium process by-products are also being commercialized. These technologies play an important role in reducing process waste, maximizing resource efficiency, and achieving eco-friendly material use, thereby enhancing the sustainability and cost competitiveness of the lithium industry. In summary, Other Lithium Downstream technologies are assessed as key applied conversion technologies for creating added value in lithium production and realizing sustainable resource recycling.
example_papers: Composition of matter used as electrode in electrochemical cell e.g., electrochemical cell, fuel cell, or lithium-ion battery, comprises composite comprising reduced compound, where reduced compound comprises particles, particles comprises grain embedded in silicon dioxide, grain comprises silicon

Manufacture of electrode material for forming electrode, involves producing aerosol from stock containing active electrode material-containing particles and graphene components, and collecting composition containing particles on substrate

Lithium-carbon composite material used in negative electrode sheet for secondary battery, comprises lithium metal core, protective layer coated on surface of lithium metal core, and porous carbon layer coated on surface of protective layer
